verses.commentary: Sermon ends; Jesus enters Capernaum. Discourse leads to demonstration.
Centurion's servant (doulos, δοῦλος) — valuable (entimos, ἔντιμος, dear) to him — sick, about to die. The slave is dear, not disposable.
Hearing of Jesus, centurion sends Jewish elders to ask Jesus to heal. Mediated request — Lukan version (different from Matthew 8 where the centurion comes directly).
Elders plead earnestly: 'He is worthy (axios, ἄξιος) for you to grant this.'
'He loves our nation, built our synagogue.' Gentile-philanthropist Roman officer. The elders vouch for him as a friend of Israel.
Jesus goes. Not far from the house, centurion sends friends: 'Lord, do not trouble yourself; I am not worthy (hikanos, ἱκανός) to have you under my roof.' The unworthy who is called worthy.
'I did not presume to come to you. But say the word (logo, λόγῳ), and let my servant be healed.' Faith in word-power.
Military logic: 'I am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I say go and he goes; come and he comes; do this and he does.' Authority by analogy.
Jesus marvels (ethaumasen, ἐθαύμασεν). Turns to crowd: 'I tell you, not even in Israel have I found such faith.' Gentile faith outranks Israelite faith.
Those sent return to find the slave well. Healing through word, at a distance, without contact.
Soon after — to Nain (village south of Nazareth). Disciples and a great crowd accompany. Lukan unique miracle follows.
At the town gate — a dead man being carried out, the only son of his mother, a widow. Considerable crowd from the town with her. Triple bereavement: only son, widowed mother, public mourning.
When the Lord (ho kyrios, ὁ κύριος — Luke calls Jesus 'the Lord' here) saw her, had compassion (esplagchnisthe, ἐσπλαγχνίσθη) on her, said: 'Do not weep.' Compassion the trigger.

Comes up, touches the bier. Bearers stand still. 'Young man, I say to you, arise.' Word to a corpse. Echo of Elijah at Zarephath and Elisha (1 Kings 17; 2 Kings 4) — but Jesus needs no posture, just word.
The dead man sat up, began to speak. Jesus gave him to his mother (same phrase as 1 Kings 17:23 — Elijah giving the widow's son). The Elijah typology is direct.
Fear seized all, glorified God: 'A great prophet has arisen among us; God has visited (epeskepsato, ἐπεσκέψατο) his people.' Episkopeo — covenant visitation language (1:68 echo).
Report goes through all Judea and the surrounding country. The dead-raised story travels.
John's disciples report all these things to him in prison.
John sends two disciples to ask: 'Are you the one who is to come (ho erchomenos, ὁ ἐρχόμενος), or shall we look for another?' Even John, in prison, has questions.
Messengers arrive, repeat the question to Jesus. Verbatim.
In that hour he cured many of diseases, plagues, evil spirits, gave sight to many who were blind. Live demonstration before the answer.

Answer: 'Go, tell John what you have seen and heard: blind see, lame walk, lepers cleansed, deaf hear, dead raised, poor have good news preached.' Isaiah 35:5-6 and 61:1 woven together. The data answers the question.
'Blessed is the one who is not offended (skandalisthe, σκανδαλισθῇ) by me.' The unexpected shape of messiahship is the stumbling-block.
Messengers gone. Jesus speaks to the crowd about John: 'What did you go out to the wilderness to see? A reed shaken by the wind?'
A man in soft clothing? Those in splendid clothing live in palaces. Not the courtier-type.
A prophet? Yes — and more than a prophet.
Malachi 3:1: 'I send my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way.' The forerunner identified.
'Among those born of women none is greater than John; yet the least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.' Pre-kingdom greatness vs. kingdom-era status — the new dispensation surpasses.
All the people, even tax collectors, hearing this, justified God (edikaiosan ton theon, ἐδικαίωσαν τὸν θεόν), having been baptized with John's baptism. They accepted John's call.
But the Pharisees and lawyers rejected God's purpose (boulen, βουλήν) for themselves, not being baptized by him. Self-exclusion from God's plan.
'To what shall I compare the men of this generation?'
Children in marketplace calling out, 'We piped to you, you did not dance; we sang a dirge, you did not weep.' Petulant non-response.
John came neither eating bread nor drinking wine — 'He has a demon.' Ascetic dismissed.
Son of Man came eating and drinking — 'Glutton and drunkard, friend of tax collectors and sinners.' Both extremes rejected by the same critics.
'Wisdom is justified by all her children.' The fruit of John's ministry and Jesus' ministry vindicates their respective approaches.
Pharisee Simon invites Jesus to dine. Jesus accepts. Reclines at the table.
A woman in that city — a sinner (hamartolos, ἁμαρτωλός). Learning he was at the Pharisee's house, brings an alabaster flask of ointment.
Stands behind at his feet, weeping; her tears wet his feet; she wipes them with her hair, kisses his feet (katephilei, κατεφίλει — fervent kissing), anoints with ointment. Total devotion.
Pharisee (silently): 'If this man were a prophet, he would know who and what kind of woman this is — a sinner.' Theological reasoning revealing his own blindness.
'Simon, I have something to say to you.' 'Say it, teacher.' Jesus knows the silent thought, addresses it directly.
Parable: two debtors — one owes 500 denarii, the other 50. The setup.
Neither could pay; both forgiven. 'Which will love him more?' Forgiveness elicits love proportional to debt-recognition.
Simon: 'I suppose the one for whom he canceled the larger debt.' 'You have judged rightly.'

Turning to the woman, to Simon: 'Do you see this woman? I entered your house. You gave me no water for my feet; she has wet my feet with her tears, wiped them with her hair.' Pharisee's neglect contrasted.
'You gave me no kiss; she has not ceased to kiss my feet.' Standard hospitality omitted; supererogatory devotion offered.
'You did not anoint my head with oil; she has anointed my feet with ointment.' Cheap omission vs. extravagant gift.

'Her sins, which are many, are forgiven — for she loved much. He who is forgiven little, loves little.' Love is the evidence of forgiveness received, not the cause. Simon's small love reveals his small awareness of his own debt.
To the woman: 'Your sins are forgiven.' Direct absolution declared in the Pharisee's house.
Other guests: 'Who is this who even forgives sins?' Same shock as the paralytic scene (5:21).
'Your faith has saved you; go in peace.' Faith — not love — is named as the saving instrument. Love is the response, not the prerequisite.
